description: Fix minor documentation inaccuracies discovered during first-time plugin developer experience testing: update install page illustration to show the recommended scaffold command, and correct the explanation of why resources/prompts require the override keyword.
qualityChecks: cd docs && npm run build && npm run type-check && npm run lint && npm run knip && npm run format:check

userStories.title: Update InstallPaths illustration to show recommended scaffold command
userStories.description: As a plugin developer viewing the installation page, I want the illustration to show the recommended scaffold command so that I don't try the unscoped npx command that fails with a 404 error.
acceptanceCriteria: The InstallPaths illustration's 'For Plugin Developers' box shows 'opentabs plugin create' instead of 'create-opentabs-plugin' as the tool label
The subtitle text below the label is updated to match (e.g., 'CLI + SDK + plugin tools' or similar)
cd docs && npm run build passes
cd docs && npm run type-check passes
cd docs && npm run lint passes
cd docs && npm run knip passes
cd docs && npm run format:check passes
userStories.notes: File: docs/components/illustrations.tsx around line 2715. The InstallPaths SVG illustration has three boxes: For Users, For Plugin Developers, For Contributors. The 'For Plugin Developers' box currently shows 'create-opentabs-plugin' as the tool label (line 2715) with subtitle 'Users setup + SDK + plugin CLI' (line 2725). Change the label to 'opentabs plugin create' which is the recommended primary scaffold command shown in the first-plugin tutorial and install page text. The subtitle could be 'CLI + SDK + build tools' to reflect what plugin developers actually install. The label appears in a <text> element inside a dark header bar (<rect> at line 2707). Adjust the text to fit within the existing rect width (176px). The install page at docs/content/docs/install/index.mdx already uses the correct command (npx @opentabs-dev/create-plugin) in its text, so this change aligns the illustration with the text commands.

userStories.title: Fix override keyword explanation for resources and prompts
userStories.description: As a plugin developer reading the docs, I want the explanation of why resources and prompts require the override keyword to be technically accurate so that I understand the TypeScript mechanism correctly.
acceptanceCriteria: The resources-prompts guide explains that override is required because resources and prompts are non-abstract properties declared on the base class (not because they 'have default values')
The plugin-development guide uses the same corrected wording
The first-plugin tutorial's 'Next steps' note uses the same corrected wording
All three files are consistent with each other in their explanation
cd docs && npm run build passes
cd docs && npm run type-check passes
cd docs && npm run lint passes
cd docs && npm run knip passes
cd docs && npm run format:check passes
userStories.notes:
Three files need the same wording fix:

1. docs/content/docs/guides/resources-prompts.mdx line 160: Change 'because it has a default value in the base class' to 'because it is a non-abstract property declared on the base class'. Same fix at line 378.

2. docs/content/docs/guides/plugin-development.mdx line 107: Change 'because they are optional properties with default values on the base class' to 'because they are non-abstract properties declared on the base class'.

3. docs/content/docs/first-plugin.mdx line 111: Change '(unlike tools, they have default values on the base class)' to '(unlike tools, they are non-abstract properties on the base class)'.

The actual SDK code (platform/plugin-sdk/src/index.ts lines 205-207) declares these as optional properties with no default value: 'readonly resources?: ResourceDefinition[]' and 'readonly prompts?: PromptDefinition[]'. TypeScript's noImplicitOverride requires override when a subclass re-declares any non-abstract inherited member, regardless of whether the base class assigns a default value. The current wording incorrectly implies there's an explicit default value like '= []' in the base class.
